speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two-- survivor, Esther Barishman-- August 8, 1995, Laguna Hills, California, USA. OK, Esther. Could you tell us a little bit more how you got extra food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go out-- my mother and myself, we used to go out through the back of the ghetto and go to the farmers and exchange for clothing. They use to take clothing from us and they'd give us some bread and milk and flour and [INAUDIBLE], you know, [INAUDIBLE] the farmers have. Also, we used to have some sodas. They were short of soda, baking soda. So we used to give them some baking soda, and they'll give us all the food from the farmers. And we really in the ghetto, because of that, we didn't have any-- we were filled with food. We had enough food. Some other people didn't but we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, given what you said before about how the Polish people didn't like the Jews, how did you get those farmers? Was it strictly a business arrangement? Or-- Yeah, it was strictly a business arrangement. The farmers were Ukrainian. But it was strictly a business [? deal. ?] It wasn't pity or liking you. It was strictly business. You brought something-- clothing from yourself. And you exchanged for food. Was this clothing that you had managed to take into the ghetto with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we-- in the ghetto we could take all the clothing we had. It's the-- and the furniture too-- as much as we-- was the-- it fit in in the room that you had. We could take everything we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. When you went out the back of the ghetto, was that dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure, it was dangerous. But we weren't caught. If we were be caught, we wouldn't be-- we would-- they would shoot on us. But we weren't caught. We-- we-- we survived that way. It was dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say that you hid your patch when you were out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding my patch not to be seen. Because, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You put it under your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it under my clothes, a shawl over it. So I had my patch in case of something, I have it. But I hide it. I put a shawl over it so they wouldn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you with your money the whole time? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to these farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you go to school in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no schools. Soon the Germans took over, all the schools were closed. There's nothing anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to continue any schooling on your own, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was-- no. No-- nobody even thought about it because we were so much occupied by staying alive to-- to rescue ourself that this was already a minor thing to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there any books? Were you able to do any reading in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, if you had your books, you can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books might be around? Old school books or other things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being a child at the time, I had some school books. But older, I mean, people-- older people then-- they took every [INAUDIBLE] they want to read. It wasn't-- you know, you could read whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you-- that you would bring in with you to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way to get written material in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no newspaper. It was just like a dead city. [? It was ?] the city. There was a few-- a few streets, you know, from, putting together wire [INAUDIBLE]. And there where you stayed. You just had to work, eat, to go to work and sleep and-- and exist-- not living, existing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any sense of the war? Did you ever hear any reports from the radio or no radios?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radios-- I think it was. I-- Radios, it was. But newspapers, as far as I'm concerned, it was no newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were some radios you think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Radios used. Yeah, who-- whoever had the radio could listen to it. But I-- I don't think there was any newspapers at that time-- not in ghetto, anyways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear the adults talking about what was going on in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. You heard what's going on. We-- we-- we knew, but not as much as it really was. Because in deep Poland, it was much worse than it-- it was in the Russian. We were on the Russian side. If we would knew that it's so bad, we would have gone to Russia, all of us, no matter what-- just-- just leaving everything and go to Russia. But it was like going from bad to worse, worse, worse, worse. Slowly, it went bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Were there any doctors in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish-- the ghetto was only Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's who provided the medical care?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't like a clinic, you know. But there were doctors. And by profession, if it's needed, he was taking care of you. But it wasn't like a clinic. He's just-- his own will as a doctor, he took care of whoever was needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family become any more religious in the ghetto or-- in the crisis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. The same.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Same-- same. Did some people become more observant or less? How would you say? Don't you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they prayed that God will help us. But that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any synagogues or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes, there was a synagogue in that particular place. And our ghetto was a synagogue there which it was before. It's not that we made special. Was a synagogue before, and it-- and it remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many people were in that ghetto [? together? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work there? Any-- oh, you did the work where you went out and labored for your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for my mother. I was still young. I didn't have a-- you know, but for-- my-- my mother had a day to go to work. Instead of her, I took her place and I worked for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your older sister? She worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked for herself too. Because she was already in the age--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was older-- or your younger sister now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a regular-- had to work every day. He didn't get paid for it. It was just to go to work. No matter where-- wherever they send you-- it's not like a steady job you have. They send you today here. Tomorrow, it's somewhere else. Wherever-- wherever they send you, that's what you had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor work-- only labor work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not supervising people or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents take this? Can you-- were you aware at all of them-- of their mood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew what's-- well, like, from day to day, it became worse and worse. You heard that this city was liquidated. [? The Jews ?] are liquidated in that city and another city. Everywhere, there was another pogrom and liquidation of Jews. And we knew not today, it's today. Not tomorrow, it's the day after that they'll come to our city the same thing. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you accept that? Did you feel fatalistic, or did you have hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have hope, no. We didn't have hope. We knew that it'll come to us. It's the-- any day, we expect it. As a matter of fact, we had-- my-- my parents had some gold pieces, like $5 and 10-- rubles, that the rubles, the gold pieces. We had a-- a belt. And each of us has a belt. And in the belt is where we hide the gold pieces in case we should have-- we can rescue ourself or do something with it. I had mine. Because I survived, I kept it until the end. But my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Died with theirs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Died with theirs.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wore these all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were keeping these on our stomach all the time, that belt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say it was material?

speaker: interviewee
text: A material belt-- and inside the belt, we put in the coins.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What kind of documents or papers did they have in the ghetto? Were there any papers that you're supposed to carry or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything relating to the work detail or anything like that? Any papers you can remember? OK. And you were all known by your real names and your real identity in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe any deportations from the ghetto that you knew about. Who was deported, when, and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the ghetto just started, they took-- even I think before the ghetto, they-- they took all the young man and the old friend, real old men with the beards. And I don't know exactly how many they catched. Like, on the streets, they catch them and put them on trucks. And they said they're taking them to work. But they didn't take them to work. They killed them on the spot. This was the first killing that they did in our city. All the younger-- young Jewish boys and the old, old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: The young-- the boys too young to work, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't too-- it was like in the teens, like, between 15 and 25-- the younger men-- and then the old men that they-- with the long beards, you know, with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there seemed to be no reason--

speaker: interviewee
text: No reason, no reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to how they were picking people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no reason. They just catch them, put them on the trucks, and said-- they send them to work. But we find out later they were killed in a mass grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nearby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearby, yeah. This-- this was the first killing. And the second one, it was the general-- all of them, no matter who, which in the ghetto-- the liquidation.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, was there any action or raid, sweep, that involved only children? You had the one with the young men-- only small children. Were there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in our--

speaker: interviewer
text: --raids of small children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our city, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right, then could you tell us now about the liquidation of the ghetto? First, well, tell us how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Start?

speaker: interviewer
text: --it started, say.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in September. It was already fall. In the middle of the night at-- at 3 o'clock in the morning, we heard shooting, guns shooting, as they shot right into the ghetto. So we already knew what's going on. Everybody was out of the houses. With nightgowns, with pajamas, naked, and start running from one place to another to rescue, to run out from the ghetto. Whoever was killed was killed. I mean, they're shooting. Whoever was killed right on the spot. We were running like over dead bodies. I myself was running over dead bodies. And I didn't-- it didn't even look like it's a corpse. It didn't matter. It didn't matter, you know. Everybody wanted survive. My mother hold-- hold my younger sister and myself both hands. Because we were the youngest children. And at once, my mother disappeared. I didn't see her anyone, and I was all by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had who with here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger sister and myself holding us by hands. And-- and one moment, I lost my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister. And I was by myself. [SIGHS] And the border of the ghetto was-- on the other side of the ghetto was a house, big house, with a basement. And I saw people are pushing theirself into that basement. So I went to and I had my half a body was already in the basement. So somebody from the inside pushed me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, oh, how selfish they are. In a moment like that, who cares how-- how many people are they? Just let me in. So they pushed me out. I pushed myself in again. They pushed me out again. So I went-- left that basement, and I went into a-- [SIGHS] to-- what is [NON-ENGLISH]? Where-- in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Farm-- not a farm, in a barn-- where rabbits were inside of that barn. And was a lot of-- of old furniture in there-- in there. A credenza, like a dining room set, which-- old one. And I pushed myself. I was very-- a small girl and very skinny. So I pushed myself in a-- in a door from the dining room. And-- and I heard [INAUDIBLE] there were a few more people in that particular place. But I didn't see anybody. And then I [INAUDIBLE]. I heard that the Ukrainian and the SS man came in and took other people from where I was, maybe like 12 people in the same barn than I was. As they came in, they dragged them out, and I was still in-- still in that place. I thought to myself, they'll open up that drawer, and I'm right in front of them. So I went out from that hiding space which is in the-- in the dining room drawer. I went out from there. And I hide myself under the-- the boards. I put boards on top of me, and I hide myself there. Two minutes later, soon I walked out from my-- from this dining room drawer, they open up the drawer, and I wasn't there anymore. If I would wait another minute or two, I would be catched too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an actual drawer or a cabinet?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cabinet-- the cabinet.

speaker: interviewer
text: As part of a dining room set?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, a cabinet. So I was hidden under that-- that board. In the middle of the night, we were-- I stayed there for 24 hours. In the middle of the night, I went out and I took the leftovers from the rabbits, that leftover carrots and cabbage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those little-- I-- I wasn't even hungry. But just I knew I have to have something in my stomach. And whatever was left over from the carrot from the rabbits, I ate it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then-- because-- after-- right after there were so many screaming, yelling, and taking out people from the hiding spaces and putting on the-- on the trucks and kill-- kills them on the spot. So it quiet down-- after two days, it quiet done already. You didn't hear the screaming and the-- from the people from-- so I wanted to go out from that place. I couldn't open the door. Like I said, I was a little girl. I was skinny and small, and I couldn't open that door. She-- They-- they closed the door from the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: The door of the barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: The door of the barn. So I found out that there's another young man was in-- hidden too and just the two of us left-- left in that barn. So middle of the night, he walked out. He went down, and he tore up a piece of the door. And I-- I went out. So you asked me, said, let's-- let's be together. Let's go together. Wherever we'll go, we'll go together, you know. So I said-- but first, you go out. He told me, you go out and look around, if there's safe to-- to be out. And if it's safe, come back, and we'll go together. So that's what I did. I went out. I looked around. But I was afraid to go back and call on him. I was-- every time we-- I looked, I thought everybody knows that I'm Jewish, a Jewish child. And they'll kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went without him. And up to date, I still have a guilty conscience why I didn't go and pick him up too-- the way we talked. But it was such-- such a fear. You-- you were-- you thought that every little three knows that you are Jewish. Were there any people out there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people out there. They didn't even think about me. But I myself felt that everybody knows that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was daytime?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were people on this farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, people on the streets and walking on the streets. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: This wasn't a farm. It was more like just a barn--

speaker: interviewee
text: A barn--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a barn in the city. It's not on the farm. It's just right outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I remembered that my father used to tell us-- because we were already so prepared that any day will happen to us, any day. So my father told us, in case of necessity, you can call on this particularly-- this Czechoslovakian family. It's a friend of my father's. He will help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember about it. So I let myself go to that farmer. And I came to him. On the way going to him, I went into a friend of my parents. And I asked them-- they had-- they had some clothing from us hidden. You know, like my mother give-- gives them to keep for-- for us. So I went into them. I said-- I was naked and barefoot. I had nothing on me. Running in the middle of the night. They woke me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have anything to wear. So I asked my-- my sister's suit. She gave me my sister's suit I should wear and gave me a piece of bread and a piece of bacon. We weren't religious people, but we never ate bacon. We never had non-kosher food in the house. That's the way-- not meat with-- with-- meat with milk. We didn't eat that. But she gave me that piece of bacon. So I show it that a Jew is eating bacon. That means I'm not Jewish. I'm Christian if I eat bacon. So that piece of bread and the bacon I kept in my hand. And I came to that farmer, to that Czechoslovakian farmer. And I asked him to help me. And I introduced myself who I am. So he-- he didn't answer nothing. He just took me by-- by-- by my arm. He dragged me. I said-- and I thought he's dragged me to the Ukrainian or the SS man. I said, please, and I start crying. If you cannot help me, don't harm me. Let me go. [INAUDIBLE]. And he drags me. He drags me right in-- into his barn and open up the hay. And there was my father hidden. So we met with my father together. We stayed with him for two weeks on that farm in the hay. At night, he let us in in the house. And during the day, we were in the hay hidden in the barn. Once, it was-- it was a Ukrainian or SS man came in and they start looking for Jews. And they were with forks going in the hay. In just close to us, we were almost hit with the fork but didn't. So-- so he was afraid already to keep us, the Czechoslovakian farmer. He said, you have to go. I can keep you any longer. Beside, we used to give-- we gave him some gold pieces for staying. But this-- he didn't care about it. We gave him just like-- we thank you for it. But he wouldn't let-- let us stay any longer. The was dangerous. It was dangerous for him, and it was dangerous for-- for his family and for us. So he-- they said, if you-- we find a Jew hidden in our place, in any place, so you'll be shot and the Jew will be shot. So they were afraid. I would do the same thing. I would be afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first family, you didn't think of-- you weren't told that could stay with them-- the one that you just got the food from. And you stopped, and you got the clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, they wouldn't let-- let me stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask them too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but they wouldn't let me stay. They said, here's a piece of bread and-- and-- and bacon and-- and clothes. But go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never ate the bacon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ate the bacon later on, but I couldn't-- [LAUGHS] I couldn't in the moment. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you and your father had to leave the barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I had to leave the farm-- see, [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE]. That means that the railroad had to be changed. The Russian railroad was narrow and the Polish railroads wider. So it was one particular spot that is they had to change from narrow to wide, from-- from Russia to Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew exactly where that particular spot-- so every-- every-- this transport that came from Russia had to stop and change the-- the railroad and go further. So they were taking-- the-- the Germans were taking the youth, the-- the-- the Russian youth, Polish too. But that time come from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took the youth to work in Germany. Because all their younger generations were on the-- on the war. And they had to do-- produce in-- in Germany too. They had the farmers and the factories to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to have people. So they brought the young generation from-- from Russia and brought it into Germany. I knew that spot where they changed the railroad. So I came to that spot, and it exactly stayed a-- a transfer with about 2,000 people, young-- young people, that were transferred to Germany. So I came up to that German, and I start crying. And I said that I was driving-- I was on a previous transfer coming from Russia. And I went down to have a drink or water.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my transfer left, and I have nothing left. I just like that. And-- and all my belongings, my documents, everything went with the previous transport. And I start crying. And the German thought it's true. So he said, don't worry. When you work hard in Germany, you will have everything. Come with us to Germany. That's all I had to hear. So I jumped on the transport, and I was already mixed in with them. But I didn't have any documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any clothing. I didn't have nothing because I ran out from the ghetto. But they thought that I ran out-- I forgot it on the previous-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]-- that's it? No, keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. And we were driving for two weeks on the transport to come to Germany. We stopped on the way. We stopped in one place in Krakow. And they registered all the people on the train because the Ukrainians didn't speak German. And they had all the Ukrainian documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they wanted to change it to German, to German. So they registered everybody on the German side. There was about 2,000 people in our transport. So I stayed. I said I'm gonna be the last one. I'll live another half an hour, whatever, because I have nothing to show them. I haven't got any documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they want a document, I don't have anything. So I stayed like the end of the line. Then it came to me they were already so tired from writing. And it came to mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I start saying-- and I heard already what they're asking and how-- what to say. So instead of showing them my document, I told him my name, where I'm born. And everything what they have to say, they just didn't even look up to me. They write it down and give me a piece of paper in my hand. And I had already a document. And you remember where you said you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everything was different, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you remember what you made up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a town near?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was [INAUDIBLE] and I was born in Kharkov. And my father was-- I told them a story too that my father was in the army, and my mother died, and I had an aunt that spoke-- that was a teacher of languages. And she taught me how to speak German. And I apparently-- I knew a little bit of German to speak. But I knew everything. I knew very well German that I'm not only by being Jewish-- because during the occupation, I was working in the-- in the-- by the German soldiers in the kitchen to help them peel potatoes and so forth and so on. So I used to bring home some beef stew with-- with everything, with bread. You know. And I-- we had what to eat. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm a little confused when that was that you were working in the kitchens.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the kitchen-- before even the ghetto, before the ghetto started. So I was working by the Germans in the kitchen off and on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: After they occupied your area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, during the occupation-- occupation. So I was working from time to time not like regular and-- in the kitchen and the soldiers, by the soldiers. And I used to peel potatoes, peel vegetables, you know. And-- but they gave me some bread and food. And, you know, I brought home and we had what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was when your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: --there was no school already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, soon the German occupied, there were no more school for anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could freely come and go to this job in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was still young-- like a kid, you know. So I was-- this was before-- before even the ghetto started-- just a short time. But that was-- even so, I-- I knew German pretty well. But the thing is too, when you don't know a language, so you don't understand, and that's all. But when you do know a language, and you have to pretend you don't understand it, this is-- this is hard to manage. Because not willingly, you sometimes answer and-- you know, because you know the language. So that's the way I came to-- to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the language you had to not know was Polish and Hebrew? I mean, you had to pretend you didn't know which of your languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to pretend I didn't know German in Germany. I had to pretend that I didn't know Hebrew and Yiddish. But this would come in consideration.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to pretend you didn't know German because you were from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the hardest part.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the hardest part from beginning. Then later, I-- I learned-- like, apparently, I was supposed to learn from them this language. And I-- I spoke such a good German that they didn't-- they couldn't tell that I'm not German. Because being young and working with young-- being in the house with young children and always German-- so I pick up the language very quick. Which-- now, I don't think-- think even I could-- I understand everything. But to have to speak, I don't-- I didn't speak since '45 German. Off and on a little bit, but not-- not constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you assumed your Christian identity-- now, this was one of the things you told them? That you were Christian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At some point, you stayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian-- Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukraine, OK. What did you have to learn about living as a Christian? What types of things did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have to do anything. Like, not religious-- because they weren't religious too, the German couple that I lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that job? Why don't we just start with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- this-- the job what they-- I mean, I came with the transport from from-- Russia, right? So they-- they're sorted, the people. The better looking, they took in the household. The medium looking worked on the farms, and the one that didn't look good in the factory. Of course, I was young and good looking. So I was taken into the household.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- this was a plus for me. Because if I would work in the factory, the-- the Russian will recognize me that I'm Jew-- not the Germans, but the Russian would recognize I'm Jewish. My accent is not the same as the Russian is. I-- even I speak a good Russian, but still, it hasn't got-- has a Polish accent in it, you know? OK, we're gonna stop here and go to the next tape. Well, that's very interesting.
